active_paradigms.idea: Make the mass-conservation plateau curve-dependent on inhibitor and monomer (inhibitor lowers accessible final aggregate mass)
active_paradigms.description: Replace the single constant plateau c[0] with a smooth, bounded per-curve capacity that decreases with inhibitor and scales with monomer availability: plateau = c[0]/(1 + c[9]**2 * inhibitor) + c[10]**2 * monomer. This encodes the physical fact that inhibitors sequester monomer or cap fibril ends, so inhibited curves saturate at a LOWER normalized final level, while cd=0 recovers plateau = c[0] + c[10]**2*monomer. Keep the existing rate structure (source + autocatalytic/inhibitor_scale) unchanged. Use constant_symbols(11). The division 1+c[9]**2*inhibitor is always >=1 (finite, positive, smooth). Set initial_values so plateau starts near 1.0 (e.g. c[0]=1.0, c[9]=0.1, c[10]=0.0). This directly attacks per-dataset NMSE because a constant ceiling currently forces every curve — inhibited or not — to the same asymptote, inflating pointwise error on strongly-inhibited trajectories. Return exactly one evaluate_expression call.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_nmse (pointwise fit) on inhibited curves that saturate below 1; also improves aggregate scored_loss since NMSE is 75% of weight
active_paradigms.cautions: Keep total fitted constants <13 (this uses 11). Ensure plateau stays positive over the observed range — since all terms are positive and the denominator >=1, plateau is bounded in (0, c[0]+c[10]**2*max_monomer]. Do not let plateau go below c at t large or integration diverges; the (plateau - c) factor naturally self-limits. Avoid re-adding a redundant amplitude suppressor already handled by inhibitor_scale.

active_paradigms.idea: Encode inhibitor-induced lag extension by making the autocatalytic onset offset increase with inhibitor concentration (delayed half-time)
active_paradigms.description: Inhibitors are known to extend the aggregation lag phase / delay the half-time, a distinct kinetic signature the current model misses. Modify the onset term from onset = c[5]**2 + c[8]**2*seed to onset = c[5]**2 + c[8]**2*seed + c[9]**2*inhibitor, and keep everything else identical (use constant_symbols(10)). A larger onset offset shifts the c*(c+onset) amplification so growth engages later, reproducing the delayed sigmoid inflection that the shape loss (half-response/onset timing term, 25% weight) directly measures. Seed still shortens the effective lag (onset already couples M0), and inhibitor lengthens it — physically consistent and cd=0 exactly recovers the current best. All constants squared for positivity, so onset is always positive and the RHS remains smooth and finite. Set the new constant's initial value modestly (e.g. 0.1). Return exactly one evaluate_expression call with the full expression.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss (slope profile + half-response timing), especially heldout_curve_shape_loss which is a primary generalization diagnostic
active_paradigms.cautions: This adds only one constant (10 total, safely under 13). Do not simultaneously multiply the rate suppression AND the onset by inhibitor to excess — the existing inhibitor_scale already suppresses amplitude, so keep the onset change small so it targets timing not amplitude. Verify the seed and inhibitor onset terms don't cancel physically (seed reduces lag, inhibitor increases it — opposite signs of effect but both positive constants, which is correct since they add to a positive offset).

active_paradigms.idea: Replace the polynomial autocatalytic saturation with a Hill-type saturating secondary-nucleation flux to bound the burst rate and improve robustness across scaled datasets
active_paradigms.description: The current c*(c+onset) term grows unboundedly with c and m0, which can make constant fitting sensitive to dataset scaling and produce overly sharp bursts on some curves. Replace the autocatalytic numerator with a saturating Michaelis-Menten/Hill form that captures secondary-nucleation feedback but self-limits: autocatalytic = c[4]**2 * monomer * (concentration*(concentration+onset)) / (c[9]**2 + concentration*(concentration+onset)). This is a fundamentally different structural region — a bounded rational rate rather than an unbounded polynomial — mirroring saturation of secondary-nucleation sites at high fibril mass (a known feature of the Knowles model at high concentration). The denominator c[9]**2 + c*(c+onset) is strictly positive (both terms >=0, c[9]**2>0), so no singularities. Keep capacity, source, and inhibitor_scale unchanged; use constant_symbols(10). This bounded flux improves numerical conditioning and robustness on unusually scaled or noisy datasets (the evaluator penalizes any dataset failure heavily). Set c[9] initial value near 0.3 so the half-saturation sits in the observed c range. Return one evaluate_expression call.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: robustness / worst-case dataset failures and aggregate scored_loss stability; reduces sensitivity to x1(m0) and state scaling that can cause overflow or over-sharp bursts
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure c[9]**2 is initialized away from zero (e.g. 0.09 from c[9]=0.3) so the denominator never approaches zero even at c=0. The saturation reduces the maximum slope, so if shape_loss worsens (undershooting burst steepness) the Hill term may be too aggressive — balance by keeping source and capacity intact. Watch that saturation doesn't flatten the sigmoid so much that half-time timing degrades. This is an exploration move: if it regresses below 0.9349, revert to the polynomial form.
